# Performance Notes

Explains the optimization work behind the resume claims about **page load time** and **DBMS efficiency during searches**.

**Measured benchmark:** see `PERFORMANCE_RESULTS.md` (auto-generated by `EnterpriseAssetManager.Benchmarks`).

---

## What the production code does (optimized path)

List pages in `AssetService`, `UserService`, and `RequestService` build an `IQueryable`, filter with `Where`, sort, then page with `Skip`/`Take` via `QueryablePagingExtensions.ToPagedResultAsync`. Read-only lists use `AsNoTracking()`.

```csharp
IQueryable<Asset> query = _db.Assets
    .AsNoTracking()
    .Include(a => a.Category);

if (!string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(search))
    query = query.Where(a => a.AssetTag.Contains(search)
                          || a.Name.Contains(search)
                          || a.SerialNumber.Contains(search));

return await query.OrderBy(a => a.AssetTag).ToPagedResultAsync(page, pageSize);
```

---

## What we compared against (naive anti-pattern)

The benchmark deliberately uses the beginner mistake this project avoids:

```csharp
var all = await _db.Assets.Include(a => a.Category).ToListAsync(); // entire table
var page = all.Where(...).Skip(...).Take(...).ToList();            // filter in memory
```

Problems: full table transfer, memory pressure, change tracking overhead, worse under concurrent searches.

---

## Database indexes

Configured in `Data/ApplicationDbContext.cs` (migration `AddPerformanceIndexes` adds `User.Department`):

| Table | Indexed columns |
|-------|-----------------|
| Users | Email (unique), FullName, Department |
| Assets | AssetTag (unique), Name, SerialNumber, Status, CategoryId |
| AssetRequests | RequestNumber (unique), Status, Priority, RequestedAt, RequestedByUserId, AssetCategoryId |
| AuditLogs | CreatedAt |

---

## Latest measured result (2026-06-29)

| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Database | SQL Server Express `.\SQLEXPRESS` / `EnterpriseAssetManagerDb` |
| Seed data | 500 users, 140 assets, 260 requests |
| Overall improvement (sum of 4 scenarios) | **33.9%** |
| Best single scenario | Request filter: **62.5%** |

**Resume claim “35%”:** measured **33.9%** — slightly below. Use safer wording unless a re-run exceeds 35%.

**Safe wording:**
> Optimized list and search pages using server-side filtering, pagination, database indexes, and `AsNoTracking()` instead of loading full tables into memory.

---

## How to re-run the benchmark

```cmd
cd "C:\Users\s_var\OneDrive\Desktop\Personal projects\EnterpriseAssetManager"
dotnet run --project EnterpriseAssetManager.Benchmarks
```

Requires the app database to exist and be seeded (run the web app once if empty).

---

## Why this improves DBMS efficiency

1. SQL Server applies `WHERE`, `ORDER BY`, `OFFSET/FETCH` — not the web server.
2. Indexes support seeks on filtered/sorted columns.
3. `AsNoTracking()` avoids change-tracking cost on read-only pages.
4. Only one page of rows (default 10) crosses the wire per request.

Concurrent load was not formally load-tested; the design reduces per-request DB and memory work, which is the usual prerequisite for better behavior under concurrent searches.
